question: How does Upwork's Job Success Score (JSS) work in 2026, and how does a freelancer's client communication and contract handling protect or hurt it? Cover public and private feedback, disputes, contracts closed with no feedback, and long-term client relationships.
answer:
Upwork’s Job Success Score (JSS) is a rolling metric calculated daily based on your contract history over 6, 12, and 24-month windows. It reflects client satisfaction, factoring in public star ratings, private surveys, contract outcomes, earnings, and relationship length [1][2][3].

Private feedback, which is invisible to you, carries the highest weight and often causes score drops despite positive public reviews [4][2]. A successful outcome requires positive private and public feedback with no disputes [2]. 

To protect your score:
- Avoid closing contracts yourself; it is often treated as a strong negative [2][5].
- Keep contracts active or formally close them; stalled, force-closed contracts create negative drag [4][6].
- Treat long-term, recurring retainers as high-value, as they provide continuous positive signals [2][3].
- Manage scope drift early by documenting expectations in the message history to prevent future disputes [5]. 
- If a project turns sour, negotiate a mutual close or partial refund to minimize the impact compared to a formal dispute or negative feedback [5].

extra_sources.title: Upwork Job Success Score Explained: How JSS Is Calculated in 2026 | UpHunt Blog | UpHunt
extra_sources.author: UpHunt Team
highlights:
Upwork Job Success Score is the single number that decides whether top clients ever see your profile in 2026, and roughly 4 in 10 active freelancers have no idea what actually moves it. The headline formula is simple, successful outcomes minus negative outcomes divided by total outcomes, but the way Upwork weights long-term clients, contract value, and private feedback is what turns that simple ratio into a score that can swing 20 points in a quarter.
...
The Upwork Job Success Score is a percentage from 0 to 100 that summarizes how your past clients rated their experience working with you on the platform. It appears on your profile under your name, it gates the Top Rated and Top Rated Plus badges, and it is recalculated every day based on a rolling 6-, 12-, and 24-month window.
...
The Upwork Job Success Score is calculated as successful contract outcomes minus negative outcomes, divided by total outcomes, then expressed as a percentage. Upwork does not publish the exact weights, but the inputs that feed the formula are well documented in Upwork's own help center and consistent across freelancer reports.
...
| Input | Direction | Weight signal |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Public star rating (1-5) at contract close | Positive if 4-5, neutral at 3, negative below 3 | High |
| Private feedback score (1-10) | Positive 8+, neutral 6-7, negative below 6 | High |
| Long-term client paying repeatedly | Positive, compounds every 90 days | Very high |
| Contract value | Higher value contracts weigh more | Medium-high |
| Contracts ended without feedback | Neutral by default, slightly negative if pattern | Low-medium |
| Disputes, refunds, mutually ended early contracts | Negative | High |
| Freelancer cancelling on the client | Strong negative | Very high |
...
The combination of public rating and private feedback is what most freelancers underestimate. A 5-star public rating with a 6 in private feedback can still drag your JSS down, which is why the score sometimes moves in directions that feel inconsistent with the reviews visible on your profile.
...
Upwork calculates three Job Success Scores in parallel, one for the last 6 months, one for the last 12, and one for the last 24, and then displays the highest of the three on your profile. That single design choice is what gives JSS its forgiveness curve.
...
A successful outcome in Upwork's JSS math is a contract that ended with positive public and private feedback, was paid out without dispute, and ideally produced a repeat hire or referral. Contracts that go quiet at the end count as neutral, not successful, which is why pushing clients to leave feedback at close matters more than most freelancers act like it does.
...
- Successful: paid in full, 4-5 stars publicly, 7+ private feedback, no dispute. Maximum positive weight.
- Neutral: contract closed, no feedback left, no dispute. Slightly positive by default but light.
- Negative-light: 3-star public review or private feedback of 4-5. Drag on score, recoverable.
- Negative-heavy: refund issued, dispute opened, freelancer ended contract early, public rating under 3. Significant drag.
...
One long-term client paying on a 90-day rolling hourly contract contributes more to JSS than three short-term contracts of the same total dollar amount, because each 90-day interval with continued billing registers as the equivalent of an additional successful contract outcome inside the formula.
...
Three patterns tank JSS without leaving obvious public evidence on the profile. Freelancers often realize their score dropped 8-15 points and cannot connect it to anything visible, because the damage came from inputs Upwork weights but does not display.
...
- Low private feedback alongside a polite public rating: clients commonly leave 5 stars publicly to avoid awkwardness and a 4-6 privately to be honest. The private score is what hits your JSS.
- Multiple contracts ending with zero feedback in a short window: when a stretch of contracts all end quietly, the formula reads the cluster as weak engagement.
- Freelancer-initiated contract endings: ending a contract from your side is treated as a strong negative even if the client agreed. This is the surprise that catches the most freelancers off guard.
...
The fix for the first two is structural: ask for feedback at the right moment in every contract close. The fix for the third is to always have the client end the contract, not you, even when the underlying reason is mutual.
...
## How to Improve Your JSS in 2026
...
A JSS sitting at 80-89 can be moved into the 90+ range in 8-12 weeks with three disciplined moves, repeated on every contract. None of them are tricks, but the compounding effect across 6-10 contracts is what flips the score.
...
A single client converted to a recurring retainer adds a successful outcome to your JSS calculation every 90 days for as long as the relationship runs. Three retainers running for a year add 12 weighted successful outcomes that no single-shot freelancer can match through volume alone.
...
Roughly 60% of bad-feedback contracts in 2026 have visible warning signs at the bid stage: vague briefs, unverified payment methods, clients with low average ratings of their own freelancers, sub-$100 budgets paired with enterprise-level scope. Screening these out is one of the highest-leverage JSS moves available. The Upwork red flags checklist covers the specific signals to watch for before spending Connects.
...
A JSS that dropped from 95 to 78 after a single bad outcome can be recovered to 90+ in 60-90 days with deliberate sequencing. The recovery is not about defending the bad contract, it is about flooding the formula with new positive outcomes so the bad one becomes a smaller fraction of the visible window.
...
1. Triage: confirm the cause. Was it a low private rating, a freelancer-initiated cancellation, or a dispute? Each has a different fix.
2. Close out any open contracts cleanly: do not start the recovery while existing contracts are still drifting toward neutral endings.
3. Take 4-6 small, low-risk contracts in the next 30 days: $200-$500 fixed-price with clear scopes, strong communication signals, and explicit feedback requests.
4. Convert one of those into a small retainer: this starts the 90-day compounding clock.
5. Decline anything ambiguous: during recovery, the cost of a second bad outcome is much higher than the upside of an uncertain contract.
...
By day 60, the 6-month window has typically shifted enough to show a 5-10 point recovery. By day 90, the 12-month window starts shifting and a return to 90+ becomes realistic if the volume held up.
...
Upwork recalculates JSS daily in the background and refreshes the visible score on your profile every 14 days for most accounts. New feedback or contract closes are reflected at the next 14-day refresh, not immediately.
...
A single no-feedback contract is roughly neutral. A pattern of three or more in a row with no feedback gets read by the formula as weak engagement and creates a mild drag on the score.
...
Yes. Private feedback alone affects your JSS even if the client leaves no public review or stars. This is the most common reason a JSS drops with no visible cause on the profile.
...
JSS improvement is mostly a volume problem masked as a quality problem. A freelancer running 4 contracts a month with disciplined close conversations climbs from 85 to 93 in a quarter. A freelancer running 1 contract a month with the same discipline takes a year to make the same move, because the formula needs successful outcomes flowing into the windows fast enough to outweigh older drag.
...
Job Success Score is the closest thing to a credit score Upwork has, and in 2026 the platform leans on it harder than ever for matching, invitations, and badge access. The formula rewards long contracts, disciplined closes, and selective bidding, and it punishes silence, freelancer cancellations, and quiet 4-star public reviews paired with 5-out-of-10 private feedback.

extra_sources.title: Upwork Job Success Score: Complete Guide to Understanding, Protecting, and Raising Your JSS | FreelanceKit Blog
extra_sources.author: FreelanceKit
highlights:
Your Upwork Job Success Score controls how visible you are in search and whether you keep Top Rated status. This complete guide explains exactly how JSS works and how to raise it.
...
No number on Upwork affects your career more than your Job Success Score. It controls where you appear in client searches, whether you qualify for Top Rated status, how much clients trust you before they even read your proposal, and whether Upwork's algorithm sends you job invitations. Many freelancers treat JSS as a mysterious black box — they watch it fluctuate without understanding why. This guide explains exactly how Upwork calculates your JSS and what you can do to raise it and protect it.
...
Your Job Success Score (JSS) is a percentage (0–100%) that Upwork calculates based on your contract history. It appears on your profile and is visible to every client. Scores above 90% are considered excellent. Scores of 80–90% are good but limit your Top Rated eligibility. Scores below 80% significantly hurt your visibility in search results and make clients hesitant to hire you. Upwork updates JSS every two weeks based on contracts completed over the last 24 months.
...
Upwork does not publish the exact formula, but through extensive analysis, the community understands the core factors. Public feedback (the star ratings clients leave publicly) is the most visible factor but not the only one. Private feedback is equally or more important — clients can leave a confidential rating that only Upwork sees, and this affects your JSS significantly. A client can leave a 5-star public review while privately rating you 3 stars, and your JSS will drop. Long-term contracts that close successfully count as a strong positive. Short contracts, contracts with no feedback, and contracts with disputes all weigh negatively. The recency of feedback matters too — recent contracts carry more weight than old ones.
...
Several things tank your JSS faster than you might expect. Ending a contract with no feedback — even when both parties agree it is done — counts as a mild negative signal. Mutual cancellations, even when the client requests them, count against your JSS. Client disputes, even if you win them, create a negative mark. Private feedback being significantly lower than public feedback is the most common hidden cause of JSS drops. And having a long gap between contracts can cause your JSS to stagnate or drift down because there are no new positive signals.
...
One of the most common and avoidable JSS mistakes is leaving contracts 'open' after the work is done. When a project finishes without a formal contract closure, the contract lingers without positive feedback attached to it. After delivering your final work, send the client a polished completion message, summarize what was delivered, confirm their satisfaction, and explicitly ask them to close the contract and leave feedback. Use FreelanceKit's Communication Templates for professional project completion messages that naturally lead clients to leave reviews.
...
The best way to protect your JSS is to avoid bad clients before a contract starts. Check the client's hire rate, their review history (what previous freelancers said about them), their payment history, and the clarity of their job description. Clients who have left negative feedback for multiple previous freelancers, have unrealistic budgets for the scope, or have vague requirements are high-risk. A single contract that ends badly can drop your JSS by 5-10 points. For red flags to watch for, read our client red flags guide.
...
Since private feedback often matters as much as public feedback, you need to actively manage the client experience — not just the deliverable quality. Set clear expectations upfront. Communicate proactively. Deliver ahead of schedule whenever possible. After delivery, ask if there is anything else you can improve. Clients who feel the process was smooth, professional, and stress-free leave high private ratings. Clients who feel frustrated at any point — even if the final product is good — leave lower private ratings. FreelanceKit's Communication Hub has templates for every stage of the client relationship to help you deliver an experience that earns high private ratings.
...
Long-term contracts that successfully close are the single strongest positive signal for your JSS. A client who keeps hiring you month after month and eventually closes the contract positively contributes to your score far more than many small one-time projects. When working with a new client who might need ongoing work, proactively offer a retainer or long-term contract. This strategy simultaneously improves your JSS, reduces the overhead of constantly finding new clients, and increases your average earnings per client.
...
Disputes are the fastest JSS killer. If a client is unhappy, address it immediately and directly. Offer a revision, a partial refund, or even a full refund for small projects — protecting your JSS is worth more than the project fee in most cases. Communicate professionally and try to resolve every disagreement before it escalates. A client who accepts a refund and closes the contract is far less damaging to your JSS than a formal dispute. For professional conflict resolution templates, check FreelanceKit's Communication Templates.
...
Since recent feedback is weighted more heavily, adding new positive contracts consistently prevents your JSS from stagnating. Even if you primarily work on long-term contracts, taking on a short project occasionally keeps fresh positive signals flowing into your JSS calculation. This is especially important if you have had a bad contract in the recent past — the negative effect fades as you accumulate new positive feedback over it.
...
If your public reviews are all 5 stars but your JSS is lower than expected, private feedback is almost certainly the cause. The gap between your average public star rating and your JSS percentile tells you this. If your public rating is 5.0 but your JSS is 85%, clients are leaving lower private ratings. This signals something in your client experience — communication, expectation-setting, or delivery process — needs to improve rather than the work quality itself.
...
Your JSS is only one part of your Upwork visibility. Your profile photo, headline, overview, skills, portfolio, and hourly rate all affect whether clients click on you in the first place. FreelanceKit's Profile Analyzer scores your complete Upwork profile against what top-ranked freelancers in your niche are doing, including JSS protection strategies. Sign up free and get your profile scored today. For a complete Upwork success roadmap, read our guide on going from New to Top Rated on Upwork and how to use Upwork connects efficiently.

extra_sources.title: Upwork Job Success Score: How It Works in 2026 - Zenlance
highlights:
The challenge is that the Upwork Job Success Score is not fully transparent. Upwork calculates it using a combination of public feedback, private feedback that you never see, contract outcomes, relationship length, and earnings weight — and the formula updates daily. Most freelancers know their score matters but do not fully understand what drives it up or pulls it down. That gap leads to reactive behavior — scrambling to fix damage after it has already been done — rather than the proactive habits that keep a score strong over time.
...
Upwork calculates your Upwork Job Success Score daily by evaluating your contract history across three separate timeframes: the last 6 months, the last 12 months, and the last 24 months. A score is generated for each window, and the best score among those three timeframes is the one displayed on your profile. This approach is designed to protect freelancers who had a rough early period but have since built a strong track record — and it means that recent positive momentum can improve your displayed Job Success Score even before older negative contracts age out of all three windows.
...
This also explains one of the most confusing experiences freelancers report: a Job Success Score that changes without any new feedback. If an older negative contract falls outside the 24-month window, your score can improve without any new work. Conversely, if a high-value positive contract drops out of your 6-month window and shifts into the 12-month calculation, your displayed score may change even though nothing about your client relationships has changed at all.
...
Client satisfaction is the most significant factor in your Job Success Score. It has three components: public feedback (the star ratings and written comments that appear on your profile), private feedback (the hidden survey clients complete after a contract closes), and the end-contract reason (how the client categorized the outcome when they closed the contract). Upwork’s Job Success Score insights documentation confirms that positive feedback on all three components improves your score, while negative feedback on any of them decreases it.
...
When a contract closes, clients receive a private survey in addition to the public review form. This survey asks two primary things: why the contract ended (the end-contract reason) and how likely they would be to recommend the freelancer to a friend or colleague, rated on a scale of 0 to 10. You never see the specific answers. The private feedback does not appear on your profile. But it is factored into your Job Success Score calculation — and according to experienced freelancers and Upwork’s own guidance, it can carry significant weight.
...
Negative public feedback. The most direct damage to your Job Success Score comes from low star ratings and critical written reviews. A pattern of public feedback below four stars signals consistent client dissatisfaction and carries significant weight in the calculation.
...
Unfavorable contract-ending reasons. When a client closes a contract, they select a reason from a dropdown. Options like “Work stopped — issues with the freelancer” or “Hired someone else” generate negative signals in the Job Success Score calculation. Options like “Job completed successfully” are positive. You cannot control what a client selects, but you can influence it significantly by closing every contract cleanly, professionally, and with a clear summary of what was delivered.
...
Disputes. Any contract that ends in a formal dispute is a Job Success Score negative. Even if you believe you are in the right, a dispute on your record signals to the algorithm — and to clients — that a contract ended in conflict. Avoiding disputes through proactive communication and scope management is always the better path for protecting your Upwork Job Success Score.
...
Open and inactive contracts. Contracts that are technically open but have had no activity or payment for an extended period can drag down your Job Success Score. If a client has ghosted you on an open contract, it is almost always better to close it — with a professional message — than to leave it open indefinitely. An abandoned open contract that eventually closes without feedback or with negative feedback does more damage than a clean, mutually agreed close.
...
| Action or Outcome | Upwork Job Success Score Impact |
...
The majority of contract disputes and poor closures — both of which damage your score — originate in ambiguous scope. Before starting any project, document the deliverables, the revision policy, the timeline, and the acceptance criteria in a message thread or milestone description. When scope is clearly established at the start, there is far less room for a client to feel their expectations were not met — even if both parties had very different mental models of what was included.
...
at Every Stage
...
Do not wait for clients to ask for an update. Send brief, professional status messages at natural milestones during a project — when you have begun work, when you have hit the midpoint, when you are preparing for delivery. Clients who feel informed throughout the project are significantly more likely to close favorably and rate the private survey well — both of which strengthen your score. The experience of working with you is a product in itself, and communication is a major component of it.
...
Consistently delivering at or before the stated timeline is one of the highest-leverage habits for Job Success Score maintenance. Late delivery is a negative signal in the end-contract reason and in private feedback, regardless of how polished the final work is. If you are running behind, communicate proactively before the deadline, not after it has passed — your score depends on the full client experience, not just the quality of the deliverable.
...
The contract close is your last impression — and one of the most direct opportunities to protect it. A strong close includes a brief summary of everything delivered, confirmation that the contract is complete, and an invitation to the client to close and leave feedback if they are satisfied. Do not beg for reviews or incentivize them — both practices violate Upwork’s terms. Simply make the close easy and pleasant, and the Upwork Job Success Score contribution tends to follow naturally.
...
### Cultivate Long-Term Client Relationships
...
A low Upwork Job Success Score — particularly one that has dropped below 80% — feels severe, but it is recoverable with a consistent strategy applied over time. Because this calculation uses a rolling 24-month window and displays the best of the 6-, 12-, and 24-month scores, strong recent performance can improve your displayed score even before older negative contracts fully age out.
...
Job Success Score changes without new feedback are common and usually have one of two explanations. First, the weighting of contracts in your calculation window has shifted — for example, a previously high-value positive contract has aged out of the 6-month window and is now only counted in the 12- or 24-month calculation, changing which timeframe produces your best displayed score. Second, a contract that was previously ineligible has become eligible (or vice versa), affecting the overall calculation. The Job Success Score Insights dashboard will show you which contracts are currently eligible and contributing to your score.
...
Contracts that close without any client feedback — no public review, no end-contract reason — may still contribute a neutral signal rather than a positive one to your Job Success Score, depending on the circumstances. This is another reason why proactively managing the contract close is important. A clean close with a clear “Job completed successfully” reason, even without a written review, is better for your score than a contract that simply goes quiet and closes without any documented outcome.

extra_sources.title: Upwork Job Success Score: How It Works and How to Raise It | AiProposer
highlights:
Upwork's Job Success Score is a rolling percentage calculated from four inputs: public feedback, private client surveys (invisible to you), long-term repeat relationships, and how contracts close. Private surveys carry more weight than your public star rating, a visible 5-star review combined with a weak private survey will drop your JSS. A score above 90% unlocks Top Rated status and the best client invites; below 80%, the platform quietly stops surfacing you in most searches.
...
Most freelancers misread what moves it and spend months improving the wrong signal. JSS rewards contract-count velocity at known-good clients, penalizes stalled contracts harder than bad reviews, and weights private feedback over public stars. This guide covers how the score is actually computed, what the common drops look like from the inside, and a 90-day playbook to raise a damaged JSS without filler work or review-trading.
...
JSS is not an average of public star ratings. Upwork has stated publicly that the score combines four inputs across rolling 6, 12, and 24-month windows: public feedback, private feedback, long-term client relationships, and contracts closed without feedback (Upwork JSS Help Center). The formula weights are not disclosed, but across hundreds of contracts tracked in freelancer accounts and community reports, four patterns hold consistently:
...
- Private feedback outweighs public feedback. A public 5-star with weak private feedback drops JSS. A public 4-star with glowing private feedback usually holds it flat or lifts it.
...
- No-feedback closeouts are roughly neutral, stalled contracts are not. A contract the client closes cleanly without leaving feedback reads as neutral. A contract that goes quiet, times out, and gets force-closed by Upwork is counted differently, because the private-feedback slot often got filled before the force-close.
...
The score is shown publicly once you have 10 or more closed contracts in the rolling window. Before that, it is hidden, and clients see "Rising Talent" or no badge at all.
...
1. Private feedback on recent small contracts. High weight, high frequency, invisible to you.
2. Stalled contracts that Upwork force-closes. Frequent silent killer, especially with inexperienced buyers.
3. Bad public reviews on recent contracts. Heavy, but rare because most clients avoid leaving them.
4. Long-term client outcomes. A repeat client ending the relationship unhappily drops JSS more than a one-off bad review.
5. Contracts closed without feedback. Roughly neutral. Useful for damage control.
6. Older contracts (more than 12 months old). Low weight, and zero after 24 months.
...
If your JSS is under 80%, the only lever that reliably raises it in 90 days is contract-count velocity at known-good clients with tight scope. Dollars and prestige come later.
...
Week 1, Triage. Stop all in-flight contracts that look at risk of dispute or silent close. If a client has gone quiet for more than 10 days on a near-complete milestone, send one closing message with a deadline: "I'll mark this complete on Friday unless I hear otherwise." This forces a closeout before it becomes a force-close.
...
Weeks 2 to 4, Target small, well-scoped contracts. Filter job posts for: verified payment method, 3+ hires, rating 4.8+, budget $200 to $1,500, deliverables clearly stated. Skip anything with "looking for long-term partner" if the poster has fewer than 5 hires, that phrase on inexperienced buyer accounts is the strongest single predictor of a stalled contract.
...
Weeks 2 to 12, Close clean, fast, and with a script. Every contract gets the same closeout message two days before milestone completion: "Here's the deliverable. If this matches what you needed, would you mind closing the contract on your side so we can both move on to the next one? Happy to make any final tweaks first." This moves more contracts to clean closure than any other single behavior tracked across accounts.
...
Weeks 4 to 12, Refuse dispute-risk work. Any client who pushes scope without change orders, goes dark for more than 5 days mid-contract, or asks for "a quick fix outside the contract" is declined politely. The cost of a bad contract in JSS terms is far greater than the revenue from closing it.
...
Weeks 8 to 12, Rebuild long-term relationships. Two repeat contracts with the same client lift JSS harder than four one-off contracts. After any 5-star closeout, send a short follow-up in week 3: "Happy to pick up the next phase whenever you're ready." Repeat clients are the highest-use, lowest-cost JSS input on the platform.
...
Executed consistently, this playbook lifts a 75% JSS above 90% inside 12 weeks in most accounts. The rate is determined almost entirely by how strictly you decline dispute-risk work during the rebuild window.
...
JSS is a contract-selection problem disguised as a reputation problem. Scores fall because freelancers accept too many dispute-risk contracts, chase dollar volume over contract count, and miss that private feedback, not public stars, does the heaviest lifting in the formula. The rebuild is straightforward but slow: small, well-scoped contracts at known-good clients, clean closeouts scripted in advance, and a hard no on anything that smells like a stall or a dispute. Run that playbook for 90 days and the score follows.
...
The Upwork Job Success Score (JSS) is a percentage-based metric that reflects your overall record of successful contract outcomes on the platform. It blends public feedback, private client surveys, repeat-client relationships, and contract-close patterns across rolling 6, 12, and 24-month windows. Scores above 90% qualify you for Top Rated eligibility and full invite flow; below 80%, the platform significantly reduces your search visibility.
...
Upwork does not publish the formula, but it has confirmed JSS blends four signals across rolling 6, 12, and 24-month windows: public feedback, private feedback (the survey the client completes without you seeing it), no-feedback closeouts, and long-term client relationships. Dollar volume matters less than contract count and recency: a $50 contract with bad private feedback can move your score more than a $5,000 contract with a neutral close.
...
Private feedback is invisible and outweighs public stars. A client can leave a 5-star public review and a 6-out-of-10 private survey, and JSS will drop. The other hidden mover is stalled contracts: when a client goes quiet and Upwork force-closes with no feedback, the private survey slot may have already been filled with a complaint. Either pattern looks like a mystery drop from the freelancer side.
...
90% or above is the threshold that unlocks Top Rated status and full algorithmic invite flow. 80 to 89% is functional but costs you badge eligibility and reduces inbound invites. Below 80%, search visibility drops sharply and the platform deprioritizes your profile on most job queries. For freelancers who rely on open-market bids, defending 90% matters most; for invite-heavy pipelines, 85% is a workable floor.
...
No. JSS is built only from closed contracts. Declining interviews, archiving invitations, or withdrawing proposals has no direct JSS effect. It can affect response-rate badges and invite frequency, but the score itself only moves when a contract closes.
...
Partially. If you pause, older contracts roll out of the 24-month window and your JSS may become hidden (under 10 recent contracts). Coming back means starting from the last 10 contracts on record. The score re-displays once you re-cross the threshold; it doesn't reset to 100%, but weak old contracts eventually drop off entirely.
...
I hide my JSS on Upwork?
...
You cannot manually hide your JSS once it is displaying. The score becomes hidden automatically when you have fewer than 10 closed contracts in the rolling window, for example, after a long pause or early in a new account. Below that threshold, clients see a Rising Talent badge or no badge rather than a percentage.

extra_sources.title: Upwork Job Success Score (JSS) Explained: How It's Calculated and How to Protect It (2026)
highlights:
JSS is better understood as a weighted ranking system that recalculates daily based on a rolling window of contracts, client behavior, and feedback data, including a layer of feedback that is completely invisible to the freelancer. Platforms like Upwork operate differently from Fiverr precisely because of systems like this: metrics that affect visibility, client trust, and access to the platform’s best opportunities are governed by rules that go well beyond a public star rating.
...
The Job Success Score is Upwork’s internal measure of a freelancer’s overall client satisfaction across their contract history. It is expressed as a percentage and is displayed publicly on the freelancer profile once the threshold conditions are met. It is the primary input that Upwork’s search algorithm uses to rank freelancers in client search results, making it the single metric with the most direct impact on earnings potential.
...
The 90% threshold is the score that materially changes the Upwork experience. Top-rated status unlocks the ability to remove one piece of public feedback every 10 contracts or 3 months, a protection mechanism that has no equivalent at lower score levels. The difference between 89% and 90% is not cosmetic; it is the difference between having and not having a tool to manage reputational damage from a difficult client.
...
JSS does not appear on a new profile until Upwork has enough data to calculate it reliably. The general threshold is a minimum of 2 contracts with feedback, but Upwork does not publish an exact figure. Before JSS appears, carefully building early contracts and selecting clients likely to leave positive feedback are the primary JSS protection strategies, because the first two or three contracts disproportionately influence the initial score.
...
Upwork’s official documentation describes JSS as based on “outcomes” across contracts but deliberately avoids publishing a precise formula. What is confirmed by the official help documentation and by observable behavior reported across the freelancer community over several years is that four factors drive the calculation.
...
- Public feedback: The star rating (1–5) and the client’s written review left at contract close. This is visible to everyone.
- Private feedback: A separate score collected from the client at contract close that the freelancer never sees. This is covered in full in the next section because it operates differently from every other component.
- End-contract reason: When a client closes a contract, Upwork asks them why. The reasons that signal negative outcomes, “work was not satisfactory,” “freelancer stopped responding,” “dispute”, feed directly into JSS as negative signals even if no public star rating is left.
...
Upwork rewards contract continuity. Any contract that generates at least one payment every 90 days is automatically treated as an ongoing positive signal, regardless of whether the client leaves feedback at that milestone. This means a long-running retainer with a client who never leaves reviews is still contributing positively to JSS through its activity pattern alone.
...
This weighting structure has a direct strategic implication for JSS recovery: a single $1,500 contract with a positive outcome contributes 50% more to the score than a $200 contract. Pricing freelance work at rates that attract higher-value contracts supports both income and JSS stability. A $1,000+ project with a satisfied client repairs score damage faster than six $200 projects with the same satisfaction level.
...
- Every 90 days with at least one payment adds the equivalent weight of one additional successful contract
- This bonus compounds up to a maximum multiplier of 8× the base weight
- A contract running for two years with consistent 90-day billing is therefore weighted as if it were 8 separate successful contracts
...
Both responses are completely hidden from the freelancer. There is no notification that private feedback was left, no way to read it, and no way to dispute it. The client cannot revise private feedback after submission, unlike public reviews, which clients can update within 14 days and which freelancers can respond to publicly.
...
Direct control over private feedback is not possible, the feedback is already submitted by the time the contract closes. What can be influenced is the client’s state of mind during and at the end of the contract, which shapes both the private NPS score and the end-contract reason they select.
...
ently Damaging Your Score
...
Upwork calculates JSS using three separate time windows, 6 months, 12 months, and 24 months, and displays the best of the three on the profile. This design means a freelancer who had a difficult period 18 months ago may display a JSS based on only the last 6 months of contracts, displaying only the most recent 6-month data, while the older damage remains in the background.
...
The practical implication is that JSS recovery does not require waiting the full 24 months. If the most recent 6 months of contracts are uniformly positive and sufficient in number to produce a reliable score, Upwork will display the 6-month calculation as the profile JSS, even while the 24-month window still contains negative data.
...
This message does three things: it signals that the freelancer actively monitors the client’s satisfaction; it gives the client an explicit channel to raise concerns; and it shifts the dynamic away from a client who accumulates private frustrations and expresses them only in end-of-contract feedback.
...
Top-rated freelancers (JSS 90%+) have access to one feedback removal per 10 contracts or 3 months, whichever comes first. Using this perk removes the public feedback and its associated JSS impact from a specific contract. Three important constraints:
...
The correct moment to use the removal perk is on the contract with the single most damaging public rating in the recent window, not on a minor 4-star review that barely moves the score. Reserve it for the contract that is actively pulling the displayed JSS below the relevant threshold (90% for Top Rated, 80% for standard standing).
...
The most common cause is negative private feedback, the end-contract reason and NPS score that clients submit at contract close, which are never shown to the freelancer. A client who left a 5-star public review may have simultaneously submitted a low NPS score or a negative end-contract reason in the private feedback. Ghost contracts, open contracts with no recent activity, are the second most common cause, as they can generate negative signals when auto-closed by the platform.
...
Private feedback is subject to the same 24-month rolling window as public feedback; it is removed from the JSS calculation once the contract’s last transaction is more than 24 months old. However, unlike public feedback, there is no mechanism to remove or dispute private feedback at any point, including through the Top Rated feedback removal perk, which applies to public feedback only.
...
Top-rated freelancers (JSS 90%+) can remove one public review per 10 contracts or every 3 months using the feedback removal perk. This perk removes the public review and its JSS impact but does not remove the associated private feedback, which continues to affect the score. Freelancers below Top Rated status have no mechanism to remove public feedback; they can only respond publicly and allow it to age out of the calculation over the 24-month window.
...
JSS behaves like a weighted index rather than a simple average. The contracts that matter most are those with the highest value, longest duration, and most recent completion dates. The factor that most consistently surprises freelancers is private feedback: it operates entirely outside their visibility. Still, it responds to the same practices that produce strong public feedback: clear communication, proactive check-ins, and contracts that deliver exactly what was scoped.

extra_sources.title: How to Improve Job Success Score on Upwork Proven Tips 2026
highlights:
The Job Success Score is not a simple average of your star ratings. It is a weighted composite of public feedback, private feedback, contract closure reasons, long-term relationship signals, and earnings-adjusted contract weights. A client who gave you four public stars and privately answered “4 out of 10” on the likelihood-to-recommend question has damaged your score more than the public rating suggests. A contract you left open three months ago with no activity is quietly counting against you. A high-value project that ended badly is weighted at 1.5× the impact of a small job. Knowing how to improve job success score means understanding all of these levers — not just chasing new five-star reviews.
...
This guide covers every factor Upwork uses to calculate your JSS, the specific strategies that move the score without requiring you to win new contracts, and the organizational habits that protect your score once it recovers. You will also find the exact thresholds Upwork uses to weight contracts, why private feedback is often the hidden culprit behind unexplained score drops, and how to use the Job Success Insights tool in your profile to diagnose problems accurately. Whether your score is stuck at 85% or has dropped below 80%, this is the practical, mechanics-first breakdown of how to improve job success score on Upwork in 2026.
...
Before you can know how to improve job success score, you need to understand what it actually measures. Upwork describes the JSS as a metric that reflects your clients’ overall satisfaction with your work on the platform. It appears on your profile once you have completed two or more projects with two or more different clients within a 24-month window. After that, it updates daily — a significant change from the old biweekly update cycle that made it harder to see the real-time impact of closed contracts.
...
Upwork calculates JSS based on your 6-month, 12-month, and 24-month history simultaneously and displays whichever of the three scores is highest on your profile. This means a freelancer who had a rough stretch six months ago but has since improved will show their best rolling window — protecting your visible score while the bad period ages out. Understanding this three-window system is important context for how to improve job success score: actions you take today start showing up in your 6-month window first, which is usually the fastest path to recovery.
...
| Factor | What It Measures | Impact Direction |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Client Satisfaction | Public feedback, private feedback (0–10 NPS), and contract closure reason | Positive or negative based on all three components |
| Long-Term Relationships | Contracts active for 90+ days with payment are treated as automatically successful | Always positive — rewards retention |
| Higher Earnings | Jobs over $250 and over $1,000 are weighted more heavily | Amplifies both positive and negative outcomes |
| Contract Length | Every 90 days with payment counts as an additional “job” toward your JSS, up to 8× | Positive — long-running paid contracts multiply your score impact |
...
The most common reason for an unexpected score drop — one that surprises many freelancers — is negative private feedback. A client who gave you four or five public stars may have privately scored you 4 out of 10 on the likelihood-to-recommend question. That private score is factored into your JSS in ways that are invisible to you at the time of closing. You see the public review, feel good about it, and then watch your score slide without understanding why. According to Upwork’s support documentation on JSS drops, even if public feedback looks positive, negative private feedback can produce a less favorable JSS outcome on that contract.
...
- Disputed or cancelled contracts: Any contract that ends in a dispute or is cancelled without successful completion carries a negative signal that directly reduces your JSS.
...
Private feedback is the most underestimated factor in how to improve job success score. Most freelancers focus entirely on public star ratings — but the private component, which consists of the contract closure reason and the 0–10 likelihood-to-recommend score, often has an equal or greater impact on the JSS calculation. You never see the private score directly, but it is factored into your JSS before it is displayed on your profile.
...
Contract closure reasons are one of the three components Upwork uses to measure client satisfaction — alongside public and private feedback — and they have a direct, documented impact on your JSS. The closure reason reflects whether Upwork classifies the end of the contract as successful, neutral, or problematic. Understanding this is essential for how to improve job success score without needing to complete additional work.
...
A clean closure — where the client marks the job as completed successfully — is the strongest possible contract outcome for your JSS. It signals full client satisfaction to the algorithm, regardless of the star rating. By contrast, a contract closed by the client with a reason that implies incompletion, dissatisfaction, or dispute carries a negative signal even if no formal dispute was filed. Freelancers who proactively close idle contracts often make this mistake: closing a contract yourself triggers the client to leave feedback within 14 days, and if they are ambivalent about the project, their closure reason may reflect that ambivalence.
...
One of the most powerful but least-discussed mechanisms in how to improve job success score is the long-term relationship signal. According to Upwork’s official JSS documentation, contracts with clients you have worked with for longer than 90 days are automatically treated as successful outcomes — even if the contract eventually ends without client feedback. This means that every day a long-term client continues paying you, they are contributing a positive signal to your JSS without any additional action required.
...
| Contract Earnings | JSS Weight | What This Means |
| --- | --- | --- |
| $1 – $250 | 1.0× (base) | Counts as one standard job in the JSS calculation |
| $251 – $1,000 | 1.25× (elevated) | Has 25% more impact than a small job, positive or negative |
| Over $1,000 | 1.5× (high impact) | Has 50% more impact — a strong result amplifies your score significantly; a poor result hits harder |
| Contract length: every 90 days with payment | +1× per quarter (up to 8×) | Long-running paid contracts are treated as multiple successful jobs |
...
If you close the contract yourself, the client has 14 days to leave feedback. If they do not leave feedback within that window, the contract has no impact on your JSS — it simply becomes ineligible. According to Upwork’s community documentation, a contract closed with no payment and no feedback is excluded from the JSS calculation entirely. So for contracts where no money changed hands and where the client is unlikely to leave any feedback at all, proactive closure often has no JSS downside and removes the ambient risk of the contract sitting open indefinitely.
...
- Negative indicators on eligible contracts: Each eligible contract displays indicators for the four JSS factors. If client satisfaction shows a negative indicator, that contract’s private feedback or closure reason is dragging your score. If you see multiple contracts with negative client satisfaction signals, the issue is systemic — likely a scope, communication, or client selection problem — rather than a one-off event.
...
### Track Feedback Patterns
...
No. According to Upwork’s official JSS documentation, giving a client a full refund does not prevent them from leaving private feedback, and that feedback still counts toward your JSS. The refund resolves the financial transaction, but the client’s private experience of the engagement — and their likelihood-to-recommend score — is captured separately.

extra_sources.title: Upwork Disputes: Protect Your JSS When a Job Goes Wrong | AiProposer
highlights:
A bad contract can drag your Job Success Score down for months. Here is how Upwork disputes, private feedback, and job closures actually affect JSS, and
...
Upwork disputes and the private feedback that often accompanies them are one of the least understood parts of the platform. The public rules are vague, the private algorithm is opaque, and most advice online is written by people who have never actually lost 9 JSS points in a single recalculation cycle. I have, twice. Here is what I learned about how the system actually works, and how to survive a job going sideways without letting it cost you a quarter of incoming invites.
...
A formal dispute is what happens when you and the client cannot agree on a milestone, a deliverable, or a refund on an hourly contract, and one party escalates to Upwork Mediation. Hourly disputes flow through Upwork's Hourly Protection program if the hours were logged with the Work Diary. Fixed-price disputes flow through Upwork's Payment Protection and Escrow Mediation, which is handled by a third-party arbitration service if the two parties cannot resolve within five days of filing. The current Upwork Help Center outlines the mediation flow and timelines at support.upwork.com/hc/en-us/articles/211062518.
...
All three hurt your score. Only the first is a dispute in the platform's technical sense. And this matters, because the JSS formula treats each outcome differently.
...
Upwork publishes the general JSS formula at support.upwork.com/hc/en-us/articles/211068518. A handful of facts are confirmed in that document and in Upwork's 2024 clarification posts on the Upwork Community forum:
...
- JSS is recalculated every two weeks, on alternating Sundays.
- It is computed over three rolling windows: last 6 months, last 12 months, last 24 months. The displayed score blends all three.
- Both public feedback and private feedback feed into the score.
- Long-term client relationships count as a positive signal.
- Clients who close the contract with no review at all count as a negative signal below a certain contract size, even with no review submitted.
...
- The exact weight of private versus public feedback.
- Whether a dispute mediated in your favor neutralizes the negative.
- How contract value weights the impact (larger contracts appear to move the needle more, based on observed patterns in the r/Upwork forum, but the weighting is not documented).
...
Private feedback is the single most common cause of confused freelancers asking "why did my JSS drop when my public reviews are all 5 stars?" It is also the thing Upwork is the least transparent about.
...
Upwork confirmed in a 2023 community post that these two private scores feed into JSS. They do not appear on your profile. You cannot see them. You cannot respond to them. And a client who leaves a glowing public 5-star review can still submit a 4 on the private survey because they would personally prefer a different freelancer on their next project, for reasons that may have nothing to do with your performance.
...
The practical lesson: if a contract feels shaky, the public review is not enough signal. You need to repair the relationship, not just the visible score.
...
The moment a contract feels off, scope drift, missed meetings, tone change, a revision request that feels like a different project, you have a small window to protect your score. Once the contract closes with a bad outcome, your options shrink dramatically.
...
### 2. Offer a small partial refund before the close
...
If the disagreement is real and you are not going to win it on scope, a partial refund offered before the client closes the contract is almost always cheaper than a formal dispute loss. A $300 refund on a $1,200 contract keeps the relationship warm and often converts a 5/10 private score into a 7 or 8.
...
### 3. Ask for mutual closure, not unilateral
...
A client-closed contract is always worse for JSS than a mutually closed one. If you sense the client is about to close with bad feedback, propose a mutual close with a small release: "Let's wrap this up cleanly. I'll release $X back to you and we can close it out on both sides." Mutual close + partial refund reads better in the algorithm than a client slamming the door.
...
### 4. Escalate to a dispute only when the math works
...
You cannot remove private feedback. But you can dilute it. JSS is a rolling average across windows, which means the fastest way to recover from a single bad contract is to close three or four high-quality contracts in the following 60 days.
...
- Small to medium size. Three $800 contracts closing cleanly is more JSS recovery than one $3,000 contract, because the algorithm appears to count each closed contract as a distinct positive signal in the recent window.
- Long-duration relationships. A client who rehires you twice within the 6-month window counts more than three different one-off clients, because Upwork explicitly rewards long-term relationships.
- No cancellations, no ghost-closes. Every contract in the recovery window needs to end with a clean public review and an active release. Anything less and you are just adding more weak signal.
...
The counterintuitive move during JSS recovery is to turn down stretch contracts. A $5,000 contract that has a 30% chance of a bad ending is a worse bet during recovery than a $500 contract at a 95% chance of a clean close. Volume and consistency beat size when the score is recovering.
...
If a client funds escrow, you deliver the work, the contract ends, and the client just disappears without leaving any review, that is not a neutral event for JSS. Upwork treats it as a weak-negative signal. The logic is that a happy client almost always leaves a review, so a silent close is the algorithm's proxy for "something was off."
...
You can nudge this. A polite message in the final week of the contract asking the client to "click close on your end when you're happy, and leave any feedback that helps future clients understand what we did" has a measurable effect. In my own data, clients I message in the final week leave a review about 70% of the time versus about 45% for clients I do not nudge. The review itself is almost always positive; the message just gets it submitted.
...
For contracts where the client has clearly gone silent mid-project, Upwork's Help Center describes the end contract process including the option to mark the client as unresponsive. That flag, documented with message history, is what protects you from the silent-close JSS hit.
...
Yes. A contract that ends in formal dispute is treated as a negative outcome in the JSS formula, and the private feedback that often accompanies it counts even if the client never leaves a public review. An unresolved dispute is worse for JSS than a clean cancellation or a modest public rating.
...
Private feedback is the 1 to 10 rating a client submits on their end-of-contract survey that freelancers never see. Upwork has confirmed that private feedback feeds directly into JSS. A low private score can drop a freelancer's JSS by several points even when the public review looks positive.
...
If the disagreement is under the Upwork Payment Protection window and involves clear deliverables, the mediation process is worth using. For small amounts or subjective disagreements, refunding or negotiating privately almost always protects the score better than a formal dispute.
...
Upwork calculates JSS on rolling windows of 6, 12, and 24 months. A single bad outcome typically fades from the 6-month window fastest, but a high-value contract with a low private score can keep dragging the 24-month calculation for nearly two years.
...
Can you remove a bad review from an Upwork contract?

Public feedback can be mutually removed if the client agrees and you file through Upwork support within the review window. Private feedback cannot be removed, which is why preventing the low score in the first place matters more than fixing it afterward.

extra_sources.title: Upwork Job Success Score: What It Really Measures | Vibeworker
extra_sources.author: Michael Watkins
highlights:
Most freelancers think of their Job Success Score as a weighted average of star ratings. It's not. The star rating that appears publicly on your profile is separate from JSS. Understanding the difference changes how you manage your reputation on the platform.
...
JSS is calculated from contract outcomes over a rolling 24-month window. The formula isn't published, but the inputs are:
...
Contracts that ended with positive client feedback — this is the core positive signal. A client who rates you 5 stars and closes the contract contributes strongly to your JSS.
...
Contracts that ended quietly with no feedback — Upwork treats these as neutral-to-positive. A client who finished the project, paid, and didn't bother leaving a review is still a closed contract with no dispute. That's fine for JSS purposes. This is counterintuitive — many freelancers assume no review means something bad. It usually means the client was satisfied but busy.
...
Contracts that ended in disputes, refund requests, or negative feedback — these pull JSS down significantly. A single disputed contract can move JSS several points if your overall contract count is low.
...
Long-idle contracts that never formally close — this one gets overlooked. A contract that's technically still open but hasn't had activity in months creates drag on your JSS because Upwork's algorithm reads it as an unresolved engagement. If you have old contracts sitting open with no activity, close them formally. It's better to close with no feedback than to leave them in limbo.
...
After every contract closes, Upwork sends the client a separate private survey. The client rates you on communication, quality, expertise, and professionalism — dimensions that never appear anywhere on your public profile. These private ratings feed directly into your JSS.
...
This is why freelancers sometimes see their JSS drop unexpectedly — a contract they thought closed smoothly got poor private feedback that they'll never see or be able to respond to. The only defense is the quality of the working relationship: clear communication throughout, realistic expectations set early, and a professional close.
...
The private feedback survey is Upwork's way of getting honest assessments from clients who might not want to leave a negative public review but are still unhappy with the outcome. Assume every client you work with is filling it out, because most of them are.
...
Top Rated status kicks in at 90%+ JSS maintained over time, with minimum earnings thresholds. The benefits are significant: a 10% fee reduction (which compounds meaningfully over a year), better placement in client search results, more direct invites from clients who filter for Top Rated, and access to Top Rated perks including the ability to remove one contract from your JSS calculation per year.
...
That last one is underused. If you have one bad contract pulling your score down, the removal option can move JSS by several points.
...
The compounding effect works because Top Rated freelancers get more invites, more invites means more selective bidding, more selective bidding means better-fit contracts, and better-fit contracts close with better feedback. The freelancers who reach 95%+ JSS and sustain it aren't doing anything dramatically different — they're just far enough up the flywheel that they never have to accept borderline contracts to stay busy.
...
Every contract is a JSS exposure. That's the right frame. Before you bid on something with a vague scope, an unverified client, or warning signs in the job description, the question isn't just "can I do this work" — it's "if this goes sideways, how does this affect my 24-month window?"
...
The freelancers who manage JSS well aren't trying harder. They're just more selective about which exposures they take. Identifying clients worth working with before you bid is the highest-leverage habit you can build.
